title: Sachis Analysis of the Sand Box Scene
display_title: Sachis Analysis of the Sand Box Scene
wikitext:
[]: Sachi's Analysis of the Sand Box Scene.             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Nov 27, 2011 1:26 am Been a while, forum. But it's also been a while since I've watched EoE. I'd just like to offer my personal analysis of the infamous Sandbox scene, and see how it matches up. So, let's get started. We open up the scene with a stage light. The stage light is an aesthetic to show that Shinji is now in a position of attention which is much like how one would be in if on a stage with an audience watching; everybody can see him, and he can no longer hide anything. Clearly the affects of Instrumentality are at work. We then get this shot of the scenery, which suggests an image of woman with her legs spread open. Between the legs is a small park: a swing set, with a sandbox directly below it. The swing set is in motion, as if a pendulum, a symbol of time.The entire park is a representation of the vagina, and more importantly the womb. Much of our beloved Freudian imagery at work again. What does this mean? Well, the womb is a metaphor for home, but I'm willing to take it one step further and say it's Shinji's world and his life within that world. We then get a cut of Shinji looking down upon an incomplete pyramid of sand. As we are seeing this, we hear Shinji saying, "... just like when I started playing the cello, I thought if I came here I'd find what I was looking for." If we'd paid attention at all during the entire series before this, we'd know exactly what Shinji was looking for: compassion, praise, acknowledge, etc. This pyramid represents himself and how he feels incomplete as a person. But the shape is important as well: it's highly reminiscent of Nerv HQ, which gives it a double meaning as the world Shinji came to when he first came to Nerv (which supports the womb being also being representative of this world). He then hears the voices of the two others with him, asking him to build a sand castle with them. As he starts building, the pendulum continues swinging. As he spends more time working at Nerv, he's beginning to build up himself with the help of others, specifically Rei and Asuka. It's important that it's these two, because these three together are our three primary pilots. What are they doing to shape the world through Nerv? Piloting Evangelion. It would also provide another explanation for why the girls appear as dolls, aside from the inhuman element it adds. But the two girls have to leave when their mother, an likeness to Misato, comes for them, leaving Shinji alone to work on his pyramid. So now, Asuka, Rei and Misato have left him. This is representative of Shinji feeling alone and having nobody to go to at the beginning of this film. What relationships he might have had along the way have died. On the verge of crying, he still continues to build the sand pyramid, as if he were supposed to, just as he did with Eva. Once finished, he stands up and looks upon what he's completed. As he stares, the lights fade and everything grows dark, and a chill can almost be felt in the air. And then he decides to kick in the sand pyramid, destroying everything in this world he's been in. This is him damning everything, and wanting the world to die, as the sun slowly fades below the horizon. And eventually, the swing set calms to a halt. At this point, Shinji has destroyed the world, killing the woman, and stopped time. This is clearly a metaphor for Shinji's usage of Instrumentality; he can end everything, the woman being representative of life on Earth. But Shinji begins building again, demonstrating his opportunity with Instrumentality. Not only can he destroy the world, but he can create a new one that fits his liking. This much goes along with the the exposition provided in EoTV. And then the scene ends. So this is what I've seen as a summary of the events that just occurred: this entire scene is a mirror of Shinji's journey through the series. He came to Nerv and piloted the Eva looking to find that love and acknowledgement he always wanted, but he didn't find it. In a rage, he decides to destroy his world, and attempts building it again using Instrumentality. The end.

[]:             Quote Post by Kazorajia » Sun Nov 27, 2011 1:31 am That makes a lot of sense. A bit more detailed than my interpretation, but probably more correct.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sun Nov 27, 2011 1:35 am Interesting read. Honestly this scene has always been a mystery to me, and this was greatly helpful. I particularly like the assertion that the pyramid represents "himself and how he feels incomplete as a person". That's a line of dots I've never connected.

[]:             Quote Post by Fireand'chutes77 » Sun Nov 27, 2011 2:22 am It's also interesting that the entire scene is "shot" using "poor quality" film. I didn't notice that facet during my first viewing of EoE because the quality of the legal version I rented from Blockbuster suuuuuucked. When I finally watched a really nice high-def version from.... somewhere, the distortion immediately stood out because of the high-quality [s:27l8itri]mind-fuckery[/s:27l8itri] animation before it. Any reason in particular Anno would chose to use that effect?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Nov 27, 2011 8:08 am Fireand'chutes77 wrote: When I finally watched a really nice high-def version from.... somewhere, the distortion immediately stood out because of the high-quality [s]mind-fuckery[/s] animation before it. Any reason in particular Anno would chose to use that effect?

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sun Nov 27, 2011 4:41 pm Well this is the best analysis of this scene I've ever read.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Sun Nov 27, 2011 5:46 pm Do not think; feel.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Sun Nov 27, 2011 7:48 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Well this is the best analysis of this scene I've ever read.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Nov 27, 2011 7:55 pm Allemann wrote: Too much "Death of the Author" for my taste.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sun Nov 27, 2011 9:16 pm Allemann wrote: Too much "Death of the Author" for my taste.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Sun Nov 27, 2011 10:25 pm ^ I think this is the most accurate interpretation of this scene that I've ever read. It should be on the wiki itself.

[]:             Quote Post by rhfxz1s » Sun Nov 27, 2011 10:55 pm That's a great explanation. Easy to understand but it picks up all the important points. My only problems are: The park representing a vagina seems like a bit of a stretch to me, but if other people are seeing it I guess I'll accept it. It's not like there isn't lots of Freudian imagery in Eva. I still don't understand why the kids are portrayed as creepy looking dolls. Would you mind elaborating on that?

[]:             Quote Post by Oral Stage » Mon Nov 28, 2011 5:38 am rhfxz1s : If you don't see the vagina, you must surely agree that the hills in the background could only represent breasts. Also, I would say the two dolls represent Shinji's attitude towards love and friendship pretty well. Sachi : Brilliant! but I don't think the pendulum represents time but instead it could be a symbol for Shinji's psychical energy. That is, when it's slow, he is feeling malaise, boredom, sadness and lethargy and when it swings he feels excitement and fun. This scene is a great summary of Shinji's broader patterns. When love is withdrawn, he reacts with sadism (which includes pretending things and people don't exist/running away, the murder of the memory of a threat), represented here by his kicking the pyramid down. The pattern holds for the scene in the hospital at the beginning of the film. Shinji can't get a reaction out of Asuka and his unconscious fills with hatred, which causes the moral "slip" that follows. But what choice does Shinji have? He hated life before her, hated it a little less while they were "together" and was facing life without her again. Shinji's problem is that his plan is: make Asuka/Misato/Rei love me, the very definition of sadism and yet, banishing sadism is actually love as Shinji knows it since his definition comes from comparing Gendou to Yui. Further statements to this effect are littered throughout Eva, the clearest being the chapter title "Love is Destructive" or else the "Beast that Shouted I/Ai at the Heart of the World" On this specific scene, the music reminds me of a nursery rhyme but I can figure out which one. It's a bit like Itsy-bitsy spider but I can't see that rhyming too well in Japanese. Does anyone know?

[]:             Quote Post by bobbyfischer's ghost » Mon Nov 28, 2011 8:09 am great analysis Sachi One question though, does Asuka calling him pathetic get worked in there somehow?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Nov 28, 2011 10:44 am Allemann wrote: Too much "Death of the Author" for my taste.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Nov 28, 2011 10:51 am I definately agree that it's intended as a microcosm of his life at Nerv but I think you're reaching a bit to make it applicable to instrumentality rather than being just a symbolic recap of the series to this point.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Mon Nov 28, 2011 4:59 pm Interesting stuff. I always took it to be a more simple statement that rage is often followed by regret. "Don't be too impulsive," Anno's saying, "you'll soon wish that you weren't". This interacts with the rest of the instrumentality sequence... Shinji fantasizes about killing Asuka, but such heat-of-the-moment violence is not what he really wants. Shinji's makes reckless, hot-blooded choices throughout the series that he's always taking back. Even at the very end.

[]:             Quote Post by Viking » Mon Nov 28, 2011 5:54 pm CJD wrote: Interesting read. Honestly this scene has always been a mystery to me, and this was greatly helpful. [..]

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Nov 28, 2011 6:20 pm When it comes to the dolls, it's important to note what Asuka says to Shinji later during Instrumentality in response to his begging for help. Something along the lines of, "you don't care who it is; anybody will do." These dolls may represent a means to an end for Shinji in his quest for love and acknowledgement; he doesn't care so much for the person, the human providing it, so long as they're providing it. They're dolls to Shinji. This is an interesting perspective which sets him remarkably closer to Gendo than I previously interpreted. It shows how incapable of love he is, and why no true shipping would work with his character. And all of a sudden, I once again feel like I'm that much more like Shinji.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Mon Nov 28, 2011 6:37 pm Sachi wrote: Something along the lines of, "you don't care who it is; anybody will do." These dolls may represent a means to an end for Shinji in his quest for love and acknowledgement; he doesn't care so much for the person, the human providing it, so long as they're providing it.
